title: Epic: vibe-seed Phase 1 — Foundation
description:
Bootstrap vibe-seed using its own process. Create PRD, DDD artifacts, Architecture, and identify spikes. No implementation until domain is understood.

## Success Metrics
- PRD complete and reviewed
- DDD.md with domain stories, ubiquitous language, bounded contexts
- Architecture.md informed by DDD artifacts
- All spikes identified and created as child tickets
- Knowledge base structure decided via spike

## Scope
- Phase 1 only: foundation docs + spike research
- No code implementation yet
- No MCP server yet (spike first)

title: Spike: Knowledge base structure for tool conventions
description:
## Research Question

How should we organize the knowledge base for AI coding tool conventions (Claude Code, Cursor, Antigravity, OpenCode) so docs are RLM-addressable, version-tracked (current + 3 previous major versions), and maintainable?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
- White-hacker agent uses RLM pattern: docs are addressable variables, not search targets
- Need to cover: config file formats, agent profile formats, command/rule formats, .gitignore patterns
- Must support 4 tools with version tracking
- Knowledge must be useful for both guided bootstrap AND ongoing development
- **Must include global config paths** — each tool has global settings (e.g. ~/.claude/) that override local project settings. Knowledge base must document these paths per tool per version so vs init can detect conflicts.

## Investigation Approach
1. Document each tool's config structure (files, formats, capabilities)
2. **Document global vs local config precedence per tool**
3. **Map global config paths** (e.g. ~/.claude/, ~/.cursor/) per OS
4. Identify commonalities and differences across tools
5. Design directory structure that supports RLM lookup
6. Design versioning scheme (how to track current + 3 prev)

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_knowledge_base_structure.md
- [ ] Proposed directory structure for .vibe-seed/knowledge/
- [ ] RLM lookup table design
- [ ] **Global config path registry per tool** (path, what it controls, override behavior)
- [ ] Version management strategy
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Architecture fitness function generation design
description:
## Research Question

How should vibe-seed map a bounded context map to executable architecture fitness function tests (import-linter TOML contracts + pytestarch test files)?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Architecture Fitness Functions as a P0 launch feature -- the core technical moat. Research into Python architecture testing tools found:

- **import-linter** (v2.10, BSD-2) -- config-file based (TOML contracts in pyproject.toml). Contract types: layers, forbidden, independence, acyclic_siblings, protected, custom. Underlying engine: grimp (programmatic import graph API).
- **pytestarch** (v4.0.1, Apache-2.0) -- pure Python fluent API, integrates with pytest. Best candidate for programmatic test file generation.
- **deply** (v0.8.0, BSD-3) -- YAML-driven, also enforces naming conventions and generates Mermaid diagrams.

**Key finding:** Java has Context Mapper + ArchUnit + jMolecules 2.0 + Spring Modulith for DDD-to-architecture-test pipelines. Python has NO equivalent. vibe-seed would be building novel capability.

**Risk:** import-linter Python API is intentionally minimal (read-only config). Generating contracts means generating TOML programmatically -- needs a Pydantic model layer to avoid string-manipulation bugs.

## Investigation Steps
1. Design the bounded context map schema (YAML/TOML) -- minimal input that captures enough to emit contracts
2. Map DDD concepts to import-linter contract types:
   - Bounded context boundaries -> layer contracts
   - Dependency direction rules -> forbidden import contracts
   - Aggregate isolation -> independence contracts
3. Map DDD concepts to pytestarch rules:
   - Domain layer purity -> no-import rules
   - Port/adapter separation -> naming + import rules
4. Design the generation pipeline: bounded context map -> intermediate model -> test files
5. Evaluate: import-linter TOML vs pytestarch .py files vs both
6. Prototype: generate sample contracts from a sample bounded context map

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_fitness_function_design.md
- [ ] Bounded context map input schema (minimal effective format)
- [ ] DDD concept -> architecture test mapping table
- [ ] Generation pipeline design (input -> intermediate model -> output files)
- [ ] ADR: import-linter vs pytestarch vs hybrid approach
- [ ] Sample: bounded context map + generated test files
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Domain story to ticket pipeline design
description:
## Research Question

How should vibe-seed auto-generate dependency-ordered beads tickets from DDD artifacts (domain stories, bounded contexts, aggregates)?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Domain Story to Ticket Pipeline as a P0 launch feature. This is what makes vibe-seed more than a doc generator -- it produces actionable, ordered work items.

The pipeline should:
- Read DDD artifacts (bounded contexts, aggregates, domain events)
- Generate beads epics per bounded context
- Generate tasks ordered by aggregate dependency (foundation first)
- Pre-fill each ticket with TDD red/green/refactor phases
- Map SOLID principles per ticket
- Derive acceptance criteria from domain invariants
- Set blocking relationships from the context map

## Investigation Steps
1. Define the DDD artifact input format that feeds the pipeline
   - What minimum structure from docs/DDD.md is machine-readable?
   - YAML front matter? Structured markdown sections? Separate YAML file?
2. Design the ticket generation algorithm
   - How to determine dependency order from aggregate relationships?
   - How to split work into right-sized tickets (not too coarse, not too fine)?
   - How to detect which aggregates are foundational (no dependencies)?
3. Design ticket content templates
   - TDD phase mapping: which domain concept maps to which test type?
   - SOLID mapping: which principle applies to which ticket?
   - Acceptance criteria: how to derive from domain invariants?
4. Design the beads integration
   - Use bd create programmatically or generate JSONL directly?
   - How to set parent/child and dependency relationships?
5. Evaluate: fully automated vs human-in-the-loop (preview + confirm)

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ticket_pipeline_design.md
- [ ] DDD artifact input format specification
- [ ] Ticket generation algorithm design (ordering, sizing, dependencies)
- [ ] Ticket content template (TDD phases, SOLID mapping, acceptance criteria)
- [ ] Beads integration approach (programmatic vs JSONL)
- [ ] Sample: DDD artifacts -> generated ticket set
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Ticket freshness and ripple review design
description:
## Research Question

How should vibe-seed implement event-driven ticket freshness detection (ripple review) so that completing a ticket automatically flags open dependents/siblings for re-review with a context diff?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background

We experienced this problem first-hand: closing k7m.9 (killer features spike) changed the context for k7m.2, k7m.3, k7m.4, and others. We had to manually groom each ticket to align with the research findings. No tool in the market (Jira, Linear, Kiro, GitHub stale-bot) does event-based staleness detection -- all use time-based inactivity only.

Research (docs/research/20260222_backlog_freshness_ticket_decay.md) identified four principles:
1. **Deferred detail** -- far-term tickets are stubs, detailed AC added just before work starts
2. **Event-triggered ripple flag** -- on ticket close, flag open dependents with review_needed
3. **Lightweight freshness metadata** -- review_needed + last_reviewed fields per ticket
4. **Context diff** -- record what the closed ticket produced so reviewers know what changed

Design decision (approved): **Approach 2 -- Ripple Flag + Context Diff** with human-in-the-loop. Agents see flags and present suggested updates to the user for approval. No automatic ticket rewrites.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Lifecycle / Maintenance |
| Ubiquitous Language | ripple review, freshness, context rot, review_needed, last_reviewed, context diff, two-tier generation |
| Layer | Application (orchestration of close -> flag -> review flow) + Infrastructure (beads integration) |

## Investigation Steps

1. **Design the ripple review data model**
   - What fields are added to beads ticket schema? (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context)
   - How is ripple_context structured? (summary text? list of deliverables? link to research report?)
   - Should review_needed carry the ID of the ticket that triggered it?

2. **Design the close-time hook**
   - On bd close, how to traverse the dependency graph (depends_on, siblings in same epic)
   - Should it also flag tickets with related_to relationships?
   - How to capture the context summary -- manual input at close time? Auto-extract from ticket description/notes?
   - Output format: terminal message listing flagged tickets

3. **Design the pick-up-time flow**
   - When an agent claims a flagged ticket (bd update --status in_progress), what happens?
   - Agent reads ripple_context, reads the completed ticket's output, drafts suggested updates
   - Presents suggestions to user: update description? update AC? change priority? no change needed?
   - User approves/modifies/dismisses
   - Flag cleared, last_reviewed updated

4. **Design vs ticket-health command**
   - Read-only report: count of review_needed tickets, oldest last_reviewed dates
   - Per-epic breakdown
   - Integration with vs doc-health (unified health dashboard?)

5. **Design two-tier ticket generation**
   - How does the ticket pipeline (k7m.11) generate stubs vs full tickets?
   - What qualifies as "near-term" vs "far-term"? (distance from ready state? priority? epic phase?)
   - Stub format: title + one-sentence summary + dependencies + epic link, no detailed AC

6. **Design the CLAUDE.md / agent integration**
   - How does the grooming checklist freshness step work in practice?
   - Should agents refuse to start work on a review_needed ticket without presenting the review first?
   - How does this interact with the beads workflow in AGENTS.md?

## Expected Deliverables

- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ripple_review_design.md
- [ ] Ripple review data model (fields, schema additions to beads)
- [ ] Close-time hook design (dependency traversal, context capture, flagging)
- [ ] Pick-up-time flow design (agent behavior, user approval UX)
- [ ] vs ticket-health command design (report format, integration with doc-health)
- [ ] Two-tier generation rules (stub vs full ticket criteria)
- [ ] Updated beads ticket template with freshness metadata fields
- [ ] Follow-up implementation tickets

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on beads supporting additional fields (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context) -- may need beads schema extension or use of existing notes/metadata fields
- Depends on k7m.11 (ticket pipeline design) for two-tier generation integration
- Must not add bureaucratic overhead -- the process should cost < 2 minutes per ticket pick-up

title: Spike: Guided DDD question framework
description:
## Research Question

What is the minimal effective set of DDD / Domain Storytelling questions to take a 4-5 sentence idea and produce bounded contexts, ubiquitous language, aggregate design, and subdomain classification?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
- Users start with a simple README (4-5 sentences)
- We need to ask structured questions that progressively build DDD artifacts
- Questions must work for solo developers (no workshop facilitation)
- Must produce: domain stories, ubiquitous language glossary, subdomain classification, bounded contexts, aggregate boundaries
- **Complexity Budget (P0 feature from k7m.9):** During DDD discovery, each subdomain must be classified as Core/Supporting/Generic. This classification drives ticket detail level, fitness function strictness, and recommended implementation approach. The question framework must include subdomain classification questions.

## Investigation Approach
1. Review Domain Storytelling methodology (Hofer & Schwentner)
2. Review Event Storming question flows
3. Identify minimum question set that produces useful DDD artifacts
4. Design question flow (order, dependencies, branching)
5. **Design subdomain classification questions** -- how to guide users to classify Core vs Supporting vs Generic, with examples and heuristics
6. Create example: apply questions to a sample project idea

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ddd_question_framework.md
- [ ] Ordered list of questions with rationale
- [ ] **Subdomain classification questions and heuristics** (Core/Supporting/Generic)
- [ ] **Complexity budget output format** (which subdomain gets which treatment level)
- [ ] Example application to a sample project
- [ ] Question-to-artifact mapping (which questions produce which DDD artifacts)
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Multi-tool config formats (Claude Code, Cursor, Antigravity, OpenCode)
description:
## Research Question

What are the configuration formats, agent profile structures, and convention files for Claude Code, Cursor, Antigravity, and OpenCode? How similar/different are they, and can we generate all from a single source?

## Timebox
6 hours

## Background
- Claude Code uses .claude/ (agents/, commands/, CLAUDE.md)
- Cursor uses .cursor/ (rules, .cursorrules)
- Antigravity and OpenCode have their own formats
- We need to know: agent profiles, system instructions, commands/rules, .gitignore patterns
- Goal: single source of truth that generates tool-specific configs

## Investigation Approach
1. Document Claude Code config structure (we know this well)
2. Research Cursor config structure (Context7 + docs)
3. Research Antigravity config structure
4. Research OpenCode config structure
5. Build comparison matrix
6. Identify common abstraction layer

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_multi_tool_configs.md
- [ ] Config format comparison matrix (4 tools)
- [ ] Common vs tool-specific features
- [ ] Proposed abstraction: single source → multi-tool generation
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Design CLI (vs) and MCP server interfaces
description:
## Research Question

How should the CLI (vs command) and MCP server be designed to provide the guided bootstrap experience, including fitness function generation and ticket pipeline?

## Decision (from user)
- CLI command: vs (vibe-seed)
- MCP server: also needed for programmatic/tool integration
- Both interfaces drive the same core logic

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
- CLI vs is the primary user interface for guided bootstrap
- MCP server exposes the same guided flow for AI tools to call programmatically
- Both must support: project init, guided DDD questions, artifact generation, knowledge lookup
- Core logic should be shared between CLI and MCP (ports pattern)
- **P0 features from k7m.9 research that need CLI/MCP surface:**
  - Architecture fitness function generation (bounded context map -> test files)
  - Domain story to ticket pipeline (DDD artifacts -> beads tickets)
  - Complexity budget classification (integrated into DDD question flow)
  - Multi-tool config generation (domain model -> tool-specific configs)

## Investigation Approach
1. Design CLI command structure (vs init, vs guide, vs generate, etc.)
2. **Design CLI commands for P0 features:** fitness function generation, ticket pipeline, multi-tool config output
3. Design MCP tool interface (what tools to expose)
4. Define shared core (application layer) that both interfaces call
5. Research MCP server implementation in Python (Context7)
6. Research CLI framework (click, typer, argparse)

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_cli_mcp_design.md
- [ ] CLI command tree design (vs subcommands) -- **including fitness functions, ticket pipeline, config generation commands**
- [ ] MCP tool list and schemas
- [ ] Shared core architecture (application ports)
- [ ] ADR: CLI framework choice
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Create DDD artifacts for vibe-seed (domain stories, bounded contexts)
description:
## Goal
Create docs/DDD.md for vibe-seed itself using the DDD_STORY_TEMPLATE.md.

## Background
Following our own lifecycle: README (done) → PRD (done) → DDD → Architecture.
We need to identify vibe-seed's own domain before designing architecture.

## Key Questions to Answer
- What are vibe-seed's domain stories? (bootstrap flow, knowledge lookup, artifact generation)
- What is the ubiquitous language? (seed, bootstrap, scaffold, guide, artifact, knowledge, convention)
- What are the subdomains? (Core: guided bootstrap, Supporting: knowledge base, Generic: file generation)
- What are the bounded contexts?
- What are the aggregates?

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] docs/DDD.md created with all sections from template filled
- [ ] Domain stories written for core flows
- [ ] Ubiquitous language glossary complete
- [ ] Subdomain classification done
- [ ] Bounded contexts identified with clear boundaries

title: Review and finalize PRD
description:
## Goal
Formal review and sign-off of docs/PRD.md before proceeding to DDD.

## Background
PRD has been iteratively built with all major design decisions captured:
- CLI (vs) + MCP server as interfaces
- .vibe-seed/ per-project directory
- vs init with preview, never-overwrite, global settings detection
- Doc maintenance philosophy
- Knowledge base (RLM) structure outlined
- File safety rules as hard constraints

## Inputs Required Before Review
- **Killer features spike (k7m.9)** — Competitive analysis identified 6 differentiating features (fitness functions, ticket pipeline, rescue mode, tool translation, complexity budget, living KB). The spike will produce a prioritization matrix and recommended PRD changes. These MUST be incorporated before sign-off.
- **.notes/killer-features-analysis.md** — Raw competitive analysis for reference

## Review Checklist

### Completeness
- [ ] All sections complete (no TBD in P0 capabilities)
- [ ] User scenarios cover all personas (solo dev, team lead, tool switcher)
- [ ] Constraints section is realistic

### Killer Features Integration
- [ ] Killer features from k7m.9 incorporated into capabilities (P0/P1/P2)
- [ ] Architecture fitness functions added as a capability
- [ ] Domain-to-ticket pipeline added as a capability
- [ ] Complexity budget added as a capability
- [ ] Feature phase assignments align with timeline

### Consistency
- [ ] Open questions mapped to spike tickets
- [ ] Timeline phases reflect updated feature set
- [ ] File safety rules still complete after additions
- [ ] No contradictions between sections

### Sign-off
- [ ] Status changed from draft to approved in frontmatter
- [ ] All reviewers satisfied (user approval required)

## Expected Output
- Updated docs/PRD.md with approved status
- List of any new spike tickets needed for newly added features
close_reason: PRD reviewed and approved. Killer features integrated (4 P0 + 2 P1), new personas added (Product Owner, Domain Expert), timeline updated to 7-phase 4+1 strategy, all sections consistent.

title: Create Architecture doc informed by DDD and spikes
description:
## Goal
Create docs/ARCHITECTURE.md using ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md, informed by DDD artifacts and spike results.

## Background
Per project lifecycle: README → PRD → DDD → **Architecture** → Implementation.
Architecture must be informed by:
- Bounded contexts from docs/DDD.md
- CLI + MCP design from spike vibe-seed-k7m.4
- Knowledge base structure from spike vibe-seed-k7m.1

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] docs/ARCHITECTURE.md created from template
- [ ] Layer architecture maps to DDD bounded contexts
- [ ] CLI (vs) and MCP server components defined
- [ ] .vibe-seed/ directory structure finalized
- [ ] Shared application core (ports pattern) between CLI and MCP documented
- [ ] Knowledge base RLM lookup paths documented
- [ ] Security section covers file safety rules
- [ ] All design decisions traceable to PRD or spike ADRs

title: Spike: Gap analysis design for existing projects
description:
## Research Question

How should vs init --existing analyze an existing project and produce a useful gap report? What should it scan, what gaps matter, and how should the report be structured?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background
- Applying vibe-seed to an existing project is complex — existing files, conventions, partial structure
- Must create a new git branch (vibe-seed/init) for all changes
- Must produce a gap report: what's missing, what conflicts, what's already compliant
- Must ask clarifying domain questions to adapt agent profiles and DDD artifacts
- **HARD GATE: existing tests must pass after scaffolding. If any test fails, roll back everything.**

## Investigation Approach
1. Define what a 'fully-seeded' project looks like (the golden state)
2. Design scan targets: docs, tooling, structure, tests, quality gates
3. Design gap categories: missing, conflicting, partial, compliant
4. Design gap report format (terminal output + optional md file)
5. Design the branch workflow (create, populate, leave for user to review)
6. **Design test verification step**: detect test runner (pytest, unittest, etc.), run existing suite, compare before/after, rollback on failure
7. Consider: how to detect existing domain language from code/docs

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_gap_analysis_design.md
- [ ] Gap categories and scan targets list
- [ ] Gap report format (terminal + markdown)
- [ ] Branch workflow design with rollback mechanism
- [ ] **Test verification design** (detect runner, run, compare, rollback)
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Competitive landscape & killer features prioritization
description:
## Research Question

Which killer features should vibe-seed prioritize to differentiate from MetaGPT, Pythagora, Lovable/Bolt/v0, GPT-Engineer, and PRD generators (WriteMyPrd, Tara AI)?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background

Market research (stored in .notes/killer-features-analysis.md) identified 6 potential killer features:

1. **Architecture Fitness Functions** — Generate executable boundary tests from bounded context maps. Architecture enforces itself via test suite, survives contact with any AI coding tool.
2. **Domain Story → Ticket Pipeline** — Auto-generate dependency-ordered beads tickets from DDD artifacts. Each ticket pre-filled with TDD phases, SOLID mapping, and acceptance criteria from domain invariants.
3. **Rescue Mode / Structural Migration** — vs init --existing goes beyond scaffolding overlay. Scans for implicit bounded contexts, identifies anemic models, generates migration tickets with before/after test verification.
4. **Tool-Native Context Translation** — One domain model generates native config for Claude Code (CLAUDE.md + agents), Cursor (.cursorrules + .cursor/mcp.json), Antigravity, OpenCode. Configs contain domain-specific language and boundary rules, not generic boilerplate.
5. **Complexity Budget** — Classify subdomains during DDD discovery (Core/Supporting/Generic). Enforce investment level: full DDD for core, simple services for supporting, CRUD/off-the-shelf for generic. Fitness functions flag over-engineering in generic domains.
6. **Living Knowledge Base with Drift Detection** — .vibe-seed/knowledge/ tracks tool versions, detects convention changes, flags stale architecture docs vs actual code structure.

## Competitive Landscape Summary

| Category | Competitors | Their Gap |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| AI Software Company | MetaGPT, Pythagora | Autonomous/complex, no boundary enforcement after generation |
| Rapid Vibe Coding | Lovable, Bolt, v0, GPT-Engineer | Skip architecture entirely, fail at scale |
| Execution Tools | Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot | Partners not competitors — need structured context we provide |
| PRD Generators | WriteMyPrd, Tara AI, IBM ERM | No software architecture understanding |

**White space: "Architectural Discipline as a Service"**

## Investigation Steps

1. **Validate features against user personas**
   - Solo developer starting a new project — which features matter most?
   - Team lead adopting vibe-seed on existing codebase — which features matter most?
   - Developer switching between AI tools — which features matter most?

2. **Classify each feature by phase**
   - P0 (launch): Must have for initial release
   - P1 (growth): Drives adoption after launch
   - P2 (moat): Long-term competitive advantage

3. **Assess build complexity**
   - Which features can be built incrementally?
   - Which require foundational work in Phase 1?
   - Which have dependencies on other spikes (k7m.1, k7m.2, k7m.3, k7m.4, k7m.8)?

4. **Map features to PRD sections**
   - Which existing PRD capabilities cover these features?
   - Which need new sections added to the PRD?
   - Which change the priority of existing capabilities?

5. **Identify feature interactions**
   - Fitness functions + complexity budget = budget-aware boundary tests
   - DDD questions + ticket pipeline = end-to-end automation
   - Tool translation + knowledge base = version-aware config generation

## Expected Deliverables

- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_killer_features.md
- [ ] Feature prioritization matrix (impact vs effort vs moat depth per feature)
- [ ] Phase assignment for each feature (P0/P1/P2)
- [ ] Feature-to-PRD mapping (which PRD sections need updates)
- [ ] Feature dependency map (which features depend on which spikes)
- [ ] Recommended PRD changes for k7m.6 review
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for Phase 2 epic
close_reason: All 7 deliverables complete: research report with prioritization matrix, phase assignments, PRD mapping, dependency map, PRD change recommendations, and follow-up tickets (k7m.10, k7m.11) created.
